questions.question: Define ACID properties of a transaction. Explain Atomicity and Durability with suitable examples in the context of a banking database system.
keyPoints: Atomicity: Transaction is treated as a single unit - all operations succeed or all fail; illustrated by money transfer where debit and credit must both complete
Durability: Once transaction commits, changes persist permanently even system failure; implemented using write-ahead logs
Partial execution is prevented through undo logs; system crash during transaction triggers rollback
Durability guaranteed by DBMS writing commit records to stable storage before returning success to user

questions.question: Explain the transaction control statements COMMIT and ROLLBACK. How do they ensure the Atomicity property in database transactions?
keyPoints: COMMIT: Makes all changes of transaction permanent in database; releases locks; transaction ends successfully
ROLLBACK: Undoes all changes made by transaction; restores database to previous consistent state; releases locks
COMMIT ensures durability by writing all updates to persistent storage with a commit record
ROLLBACK ensures atomicity by executing undo operations using log records when transaction fails or is explicitly aborted

questions.question: Discuss the concurrency problems that can occur when multiple transactions execute concurrently. Explain how different Isolation levels (READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE) help in preventing these problems.
keyPoints: Lost Update: Two transactions update same data simultaneously; one update overwrites other - prevented by locking
Dirty Read: Transaction reads uncommitted data from another transaction that later rolls back - prevented by READ COMMITTED and above
Non-Repeatable Read: Transaction reads same data twice but gets different values due to concurrent update - prevented by REPEATABLE READ
Phantom Read: Transaction re-executes query but sees new rows due to concurrent insert/delete - prevented by SERIALIZABLE (range locks)

questions.question: Explain the concept of Serializability in transaction scheduling. Differentiate between Conflict Serializability and View Serializability. Also explain how the DBMS ensures serializable schedules through locking protocols.
keyPoints: Serializability: Schedule produces same result as some serial execution of transactions; ensures correctness despite concurrency
Conflict Serializability: Operations conflict if they access same item and at least one is write; reorderable if no conflicts in cycle - checked via precedence graph
View Serializability: Equivalent to some serial schedule in terms of read-from relationships; broader than conflict serializability but NP-complete to check
Two-Phase Locking (2PL) ensures conflict serializability: growing phase (acquire locks) and shrinking phase (release locks); strict 2PL holds locks until commit/rollback
